quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Mass-spring system"?

options.text: s = rθ; the arc length swept by a point at radius r when the object rotates through angle θ (in radians)
The change in position vector Δx = x_final - x_initial; a signed quantity indicating direction, measured in meters
A mass m on a spring with constant k oscillates with ω = √(k/m) and T = 2π√(m/k)
A force whose work is path-independent; equivalently, the work around any closed path is zero; examples: gravity, spring force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Mass-spring system" is defined as: A mass m on a spring with constant k oscillates with ω = √(k/m) and T = 2π√(m/k). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Wave equation"?

options.text: P = F·v = Fv cosθ; the power delivered at a specific instant when force F acts on an object moving at velocity v
A·B = ABcosθ = AxBx + AyBy; a scalar measuring the projection of one vector onto another; used in calculating work
The gravitational force on an object is the vector sum of the gravitational forces from all other masses
y(x,t) = A sin(kx - ωt); describes a sinusoidal traveling wave with wave number k = 2π/λ and angular frequency ω = 2πf

quiz_questions.explanation: "Wave equation" is defined as: y(x,t) = A sin(kx - ωt); describes a sinusoidal traveling wave with wave number k = 2π/λ and angular frequency ω = 2πf. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Wave speed"?

options.text: τ = r × F; magnitude τ = rF sinθ; the rotational effectiveness of a force, measured in N·m
F_net = ma or F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals mass times acceleration, or equivalently the rate of change of momentum
Circular motion at constant speed; acceleration is purely centripetal (inward) with magnitude v²/r
v = λf = ω/k; the speed at which the wave pattern propagates through the medium

quiz_questions.explanation: "Wave speed" is defined as: v = λf = ω/k; the speed at which the wave pattern propagates through the medium. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Transverse vs. longitudinal"?

options.text: r⊥ = r sinθ; the perpendicular distance from the pivot point to the line of action of the force
Transverse: displacement perpendicular to wave direction (e.g., string); Longitudinal: displacement parallel (e.g., sound)
E = KE + PE = ½mv² - GMm/r = -GMm/(2r) for circular orbits; negative means bound
ω = dθ/dt; measured in rad/s; related to linear velocity by v = rω

quiz_questions.explanation: "Transverse vs. longitudinal" is defined as: Transverse: displacement perpendicular to wave direction (e.g., string); Longitudinal: displacement parallel (e.g., sound). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Superposition"?

options.text: F_c = mv²/r; the net radial force needed to maintain circular motion; always directed toward the center
A×B; a vector perpendicular to both A and B with magnitude ABsinθ; used in calculating torque and magnetic force
KE_rot = ½Iω²; energy due to rotation, analogous to ½mv² for translation
When two waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference and standing waves

quiz_questions.explanation: "Superposition" is defined as: When two waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference and standing waves. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A mass m on a spring with constant k oscillates with ω = √(k/m) and T = 2π√(m/k)"?

options.text: Mass-spring system
Non-conservative work
Normal force on incline
Unit vector

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Mass-spring system".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "y(x,t) = A sin(kx - ωt); describes a sinusoidal traveling wave with wave number k = 2π/λ and angular frequency ω = 2πf"?

options.text: Vertical circle dynamics
Wave equation
Hooke's Law
Elastic PE

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Wave equation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "v = λf = ω/k; the speed at which the wave pattern propagates through the medium"?

options.text: Angular frequency
Gravitational field
Perfectly inelastic collision
Wave speed

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Wave speed".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Transverse: displacement perpendicular to wave direction (e.g., string); Longitudinal: displacement parallel (e.g., sound)"?

options.text: Tangential velocity
Net force
Perfectly inelastic collision
Transverse vs. longitudinal

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Transverse vs. longitudinal".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "When two waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference and standing waves"?

options.text: Angular impulse
Superposition
Friction as a non-conservative force
Horsepower

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Superposition".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Springs and Wave Motion is accurate?

options.text: Force-potential energy diagrams reveal equilibrium positions (where F = -dU/dx = 0) and their stability (slope determines restoring or repelling character).
Conservative forces (gravity, springs) have associated potential energy functions, while non-conservative forces (friction) convert mechanical energy to thermal energy.
The coefficient of restitution e = (v₂' - v₁')/(v₁ - v₂) quantifies the elasticity of a collision (e = 1 elastic, e = 0 perfectly inelastic).
Springs and Wave Motion connect oscillating springs to the fundamental properties of waves.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Springs and Wave Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Springs and Wave Motion?

options.text: A mass on a spring executing SHM with ω = √(k/m) demonstrates how periodic oscillations at a source generate traveling waves when coupled to a medium.
Standard formulas exist for common shapes (rod, disk, sphere, hoop) that appear frequently on the AP exam.
The potential energy function U(x) stores energy that can be recovered as kinetic energy.
For an isolated system, the center of mass velocity is constant regardless of internal interactions, making it invaluable for analyzing explosions and multi-body systems.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Springs and Wave Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Springs and Wave Motion?

options.text: Universal Gravitation states that every mass attracts every other mass with force F = Gm₁m₂/r², where G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².
Wave properties—wavelength λ, frequency f, speed v = λf, and amplitude—are described mathematically by y(x,t) = A sin(kx - ωt), linking oscillation parameters to wave propagation.

Energy conservation with U = -GMm/r is essential for orbit analysis: total mechanical energy E = ½mv² - GMm/r determines whether an orbit is bound (E < 0) or unbound (E ≥ 0), and escape velocity follows from setting E = 0.
Gravitational Potential Energy in the universal form is U = -GMm/r, where r is the distance between centers of mass.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Springs and Wave Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Springs and Wave Motion?

options.text: Rotational Inertia (moment of inertia, I) quantifies an object's resistance to angular acceleration, analogous to mass in linear motion.
Work and Energy provide a scalar framework for analyzing motion without tracking forces and accelerations over time.

Conservation of momentum applies to collisions, explosions, and recoil in one and two dimensions, and is particularly powerful when forces are impulsive (large and brief) or when the system is isolated.
Springs and Wave Motion connect oscillating springs to the fundamental properties of waves.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Springs and Wave Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Springs and Wave Motion?

options.text: The parallel axis theorem I = I_cm + Md² relates the moment of inertia about any axis to that about the center of mass.
Forces and Motion applies Newton's laws to systems with multiple forces, including tension, normal force, applied forces, and weight.
A mass on a spring executing SHM with ω = √(k/m) demonstrates how periodic oscillations at a source generate traveling waves when coupled to a medium.
When net external torque is zero, angular momentum is conserved: L_i = L_f (I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Springs and Wave Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Springs and Wave Motion?

options.text: Calculating the mass of Earth from the known value of g at the surface and Earth's radius: M = gR²/G
SpaceX and NASA calculating orbital insertion burns using v = √(GM/r) to place satellites at specific altitudes and orbital velocities
Musical instrument design: string tension and density determine wave speed and thus the fundamental frequency of vibrating strings
Rocket propulsion using F = dp/dt with changing mass: thrust = (dm/dt)·v_exhaust, relating exhaust velocity and fuel burn rate to propulsive force

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Springs and Wave Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Springs and Wave Motion?

options.text: Automotive engineering placing a vehicle's center of mass low and centrally to optimize handling, stability, and rollover resistance
Ultrasound imaging using the wave equation v = λf to determine how ultrasound wave properties affect resolution and penetration depth in tissue
Space elevator feasibility studies computing the potential energy change of lifting payload from Earth's surface to geosynchronous altitude versus traditional rocket propulsion
Orbital mechanics where gravitational force provides the centripetal force: mg = mv²/r leads to orbital velocity v = √(gr) for low orbits

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Springs and Wave Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Springs and Wave Motion applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Foucault pendulum demonstration proving Earth's rotation by observing the slow precession of a long pendulum's swing plane over hours
Automotive suspension system analysis modeling shock absorbers as damped SHM to optimize ride comfort and handling characteristics
Orbital mechanics where gravitational force provides the centripetal force: mg = mv²/r leads to orbital velocity v = √(gr) for low orbits
Earthquake engineering analyzing seismic wave propagation (P-waves longitudinal, S-waves transverse) through different geological layers

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Springs and Wave Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Springs and Wave Motion?

options.text: Musical instrument design: string tension and density determine wave speed and thus the fundamental frequency of vibrating strings
Automotive engineering designing braking systems by analyzing deceleration forces and stopping distances using F = ma
Nuclear physics analyzing particle collision products using conservation of momentum in two dimensions to identify unknown particles
Orbital mechanics where gravitational force provides the centripetal force: mg = mv²/r leads to orbital velocity v = √(gr) for low orbits

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Springs and Wave Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Springs and Wave Motion in practice?

options.text: Computer graphics and game physics engines using dot and cross products for collision detection, lighting calculations, and surface normal computations
Neutron moderation in nuclear reactors: elastic collisions between neutrons and hydrogen atoms (equal mass) transfer maximum kinetic energy in a single collision
Ice skater spins: decreasing I by pulling arms in causes ω to increase to conserve L; extending arms slows the spin
Ultrasound imaging using the wave equation v = λf to determine how ultrasound wave properties affect resolution and penetration depth in tissue

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Springs and Wave Motion.
